items.q: What does an NMR chemical shift (δ, in ppm) actually measure?
items.correct: The position of a peak RELATIVE to TMS (tetramethylsilane, δ = 0), normalised by the spectrometer frequency.
items.misconception: Chemical shift is the absolute resonance frequency of the nucleus in Hz.
items.random_wrong: Chemical shift is the mass of the molecule divided by 1000.
items.why: Students see ppm and think it's an absolute scale, not realising it's a frequency-independent ratio against a reference.

items.q: What kind of bond forms between a ligand and a central metal ion in a complex?
items.correct: A dative (coordinate) covalent bond — the ligand donates a lone pair into an empty orbital on the metal.
items.misconception: The ligand forms an ionic bond with the metal because the metal is positively charged.
items.random_wrong: The ligand shares electrons equally with the metal as a normal covalent bond.
items.why: Students see the +ve metal and assume electrostatic ionic bonding, missing that ligands donate lone pairs into vacant d-orbitals.

items.q: How does a fuel cell differ from a conventional battery?
items.correct: A fuel cell continuously consumes externally supplied fuel (e.g. hydrogen) and oxidant; a battery has a fixed amount of reactants that get used up.
items.misconception: Fuel cells and batteries are identical — both just store electrical energy.
items.random_wrong: Fuel cells burn fuel to generate heat which is then converted to electricity.
items.why: Students think 'fuel' implies combustion; they miss that fuel cells generate electricity directly from redox reactions, continuously refuelled.
Correct answer: Gillespie and Nyholm 1957. “Lewis 1916” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Lewis dot structures were proposed in:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1916. “1869” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The real figure is 109.5 degrees. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
